title: Founding of the People's Republic of China
summary: On 1 October 1949, from a rostrum atop the Gate of Heavenly Peace overlooking Tiananmen Square in Beijing, Mao Zedong proclaimed the founding of the People's Republic of China. A ceremony of some 300,000 people, a military parade of Red Army units, and the raising of a new five-star flag marked the end of nearly forty years of republican fragmentation, warlordism, foreign invasion, and civil war. Within weeks the last remnants of Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government fled to Taiwan. The new state, the world's most populous country, would reshape Asia and the Cold War over the second half of the twentieth century.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The People's Republic was the product of a struggle that had begun with the fall of the Qing dynasty in the revolution of 1911-12 and the founding of the Republic of China under Sun Yat-sen. In the two decades that followed, republican rule dissolved into warlord fiefdoms, and Sun's Kuomintang (KMT), reorganised in 1923 with Soviet advice, formed a united front with the newly founded Chinese Communist Party (CCP) to reunite the country. The Northern Expedition of 1926-28, led after Sun's death by Chiang Kai-shek, largely accomplished that mission on paper, but in April 1927 Chiang turned on his communist allies in Shanghai and other cities in a bloody purge that drove them into the countryside.

Through the 1930s the Communists reorganised in rural base areas. Encircled and driven out of Jiangxi by Nationalist campaigns, they undertook the Long March of 1934-35, a fighting retreat of about 9,000 kilometres to Yan'an in Shaanxi, in the course of which Mao Zedong emerged as the party's dominant figure. The Japanese invasion of 1937 forced a second united front. For eight years the CCP built its strength behind Japanese lines, expanding its army from about 40,000 to over 900,000 and its territory to some 100 million people, while the Nationalists fought the main conventional war at heavy cost. By the time Japan surrendered in August 1945, the two rival Chinese armies were much closer in size than in 1937.

The civil war resumed in full in the summer of 1946. American attempts at mediation, first by Ambassador Patrick Hurley and then by General George Marshall, collapsed. Chiang initially had numerical and material superiority and captured Yan'an in March 1947, but Communist commanders including Lin Biao in Manchuria, Chen Yi and Deng Xiaoping in central China, and Peng Dehuai in the north-west, aided by land reform that rallied peasants and by the capture of vast Japanese arms stocks in Manchuria, turned the tide in 1948. The battle of Liaoshen in autumn 1948 gave the Communists Manchuria; the battle of Huai-Hai in November 1948-January 1949 destroyed much of the Nationalist army in central China; the battle of Pingjin took Beijing peacefully on 31 January 1949. In April 1949 the People's Liberation Army crossed the Yangtze and took the Nationalist capital Nanjing; Shanghai fell in May, Wuhan in June, Xi'an in early October. Chiang moved his government first to Guangzhou, then to Chongqing, and, on 7 December 1949, to Taiwan.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Preparations for the founding of the new state began well before the ceremony in Tiananmen. In September 1949 the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) met at Zhongnanhai in Beijing, then still called Beiping, drawing together 662 delegates from the CCP, small non-communist parties, mass organisations, minority nationalities, and prominent independents. Between 21 and 30 September the conference adopted the Common Programme, a provisional constitution proclaiming a 'new democracy' under communist leadership; elected a Central People's Government Council with Mao Zedong as chairman; adopted the five-star red flag as the national flag; chose the March of the Volunteers as the provisional national anthem; and voted to move the capital from Nanjing to Beijing, which reverted to its old name.

On the afternoon of 1 October 1949 Mao and other party leaders climbed the steps of the Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen), from which Ming and Qing emperors had once issued decrees. At three o'clock, before an assembled crowd of some 300,000 in the square below, Mao stepped to a microphone and, in his Hunanese accent, announced that 'the Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China is founded today'. Beneath him the new flag was raised for the first time. The Common Programme's principal officers, Zhou Enlai as premier and foreign minister, Zhu De as commander-in-chief of the People's Liberation Army, and Vice-Chairmen including Song Qingling (Sun Yat-sen's widow) and Zhang Lan, took their oaths.

A military parade followed, led by units of the People's Liberation Army with a mix of captured American, Japanese, and Soviet equipment; small formations of the fledgling People's Liberation Army Air Force flew overhead. The evening ended with fireworks over Tiananmen. In the following days the Soviet Union became the first foreign state to recognise the new government (2 October), followed by the other people's democracies of eastern Europe, then by India (December 1949), Britain (January 1950), and, more slowly, most of the non-American world. The United States did not extend recognition until 1979. In Taiwan, Chiang Kai-shek's Republic of China government continued to claim to be the legitimate ruler of all China and, until 1971, occupied the Chinese seat at the United Nations.

image.caption: Mao Zedong proclaims the founding of the People's Republic of China from the Gate of Heavenly Peace, 1 October 1949.
image.credit: Hou Bo, Xinhua, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The new government moved rapidly to consolidate power. Between late 1949 and 1952 it launched land reform in the countryside, expropriating landlord holdings and redistributing them to peasants; the movement was accompanied by mass mobilisation and the execution of an estimated one to two million people classified as landlords or counter-revolutionaries. In the cities, campaigns against 'bandits, spies, and reactionaries' and against corruption and waste (the 'Three-Anti' and 'Five-Anti' movements of 1951-52) tightened party control. In February 1950 Mao's long visit to Moscow produced a Sino-Soviet Treaty of Friendship, Alliance and Mutual Assistance and Soviet aid for a first Chinese Five-Year Plan modelled on the Soviet template.

In June 1950 the outbreak of the Korean War drew the young People's Republic into a war with the United States before its own economy had recovered. Chinese People's Volunteers under Peng Dehuai crossed the Yalu in October 1950 and fought UN forces to a stalemate along the 38th parallel; the war, ending in armistice in July 1953, cost China more than 100,000 dead but established the PRC as a serious military power. Taiwan, protected by the American Seventh Fleet from June 1950, became a permanent point of contention.

Domestically the following decades were a whirl of mobilisation. The First Five-Year Plan of 1953-57 produced rapid heavy industrial growth. The Great Leap Forward launched in 1958, an attempt at mass collectivisation and backyard steel production, produced instead a catastrophic famine that killed an estimated 15 to 45 million people between 1959 and 1961. The Cultural Revolution of 1966-76, launched by Mao to purify the revolution and settle scores with rivals, killed and displaced many millions more and set back Chinese universities, science, and administration by a generation. After Mao's death in September 1976, the arrest of the Gang of Four, and the return to power of Deng Xiaoping in 1978, the PRC embarked on reform and opening under the slogan of 'socialism with Chinese characteristics', producing four decades of economic growth on a scale unprecedented in history.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The founding of the People's Republic remains, in official Chinese memory, the day on which the Chinese people 'stood up' after a century of what is called the 'century of humiliation' at the hands of foreign powers. National Day on 1 October is marked by parades, fireworks, and a week-long holiday during which hundreds of millions of Chinese travel; the 70th anniversary parade in 2019 was among the largest ever staged. The image of Mao at the microphone above Tiananmen Square remains one of the twentieth century's iconic photographs, and his portrait still hangs on the gate itself.

Internationally the PRC has been one of the great shapers of contemporary world politics. Its early revolutionary alliance with Moscow gave way to the Sino-Soviet split of the late 1950s and 1960s and to armed clashes on the Ussuri River in 1969; the American opening under Richard Nixon in 1972 and the diplomatic recognition of 1979 realigned the Cold War; the PRC replaced the Republic of China at the United Nations in 1971 and inherited its permanent seat on the Security Council. Since the late 1970s Chinese economic growth has lifted several hundred million people out of extreme poverty, made China the world's largest exporter and second-largest economy, and reshaped supply chains, commodity markets, and geopolitics from Africa to Latin America to the Indo-Pacific.

Internally the party-state that Mao founded has proved remarkably durable, surviving the crises of the Cultural Revolution, the demonstrations and crackdown at Tiananmen Square in June 1989, and the collapse of every other communist regime except a handful in Cuba, Vietnam, North Korea, and Laos. Under Xi Jinping since 2012 it has centralised power, expanded internal surveillance, tightened control over Hong Kong and the Xinjiang region, and pressed harder on its claims to Taiwan and the South China Sea. Whatever one's view of its politics, the state proclaimed at Tiananmen on 1 October 1949 is now, three quarters of a century later, one of the defining actors of the twenty-first-century world.

image.caption: The Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen) in Beijing, from which the People's Republic was proclaimed on 1 October 1949.

worldContext.text: The United States in October 1949 was still absorbing the shock of the Soviet detonation of its first atomic bomb on 29 August, announced by President Harry Truman on 23 September. The 'loss' of China would soon become a central theme of American domestic politics and helped launch Senator Joseph McCarthy's anti-communist campaign in early 1950. NATO, whose founding treaty had been signed in Washington on 4 April 1949, was still ratifying instruments. In Latin America, Juan Peron's Argentina and Getulio Vargas's Brazil were rebuilding postwar economies around import substitution.

Europe in October 1949 was dividing. The Federal Republic of Germany had been proclaimed at Bonn in May and Konrad Adenauer had become its first chancellor in September; the German Democratic Republic in the Soviet zone was formally proclaimed on 7 October 1949. The Berlin Airlift had ended in May after the Soviet blockade was lifted. The Marshall Plan was pouring aid into western Europe. In eastern Europe, Stalinist trials against 'Titoist' communists were under way in Bulgaria, Hungary, and elsewhere. Britain, exhausted, was heading for a devaluation of sterling.

India, now two years independent under Jawaharlal Nehru, was moving to recognise the People's Republic (which it did on 30 December 1949) and framing its own constitution, promulgated on 26 January 1950. The first India-Pakistan war over Kashmir had ended in a UN-brokered ceasefire in January 1949; a UN Commission on India and Pakistan was operating along the ceasefire line. In Ceylon (Sri Lanka), independent since 1948, D. S. Senanayake's government negotiated a defence agreement with Britain. Afghanistan under King Zahir Shah pursued a cautious neutralism.

The Middle East in October 1949 was still absorbing the consequences of the previous year's Arab-Israeli war. Armistices between Israel and Egypt, Jordan, Lebanon, and Syria signed on Rhodes in early 1949 had left roughly 700,000 Palestinian refugees displaced, mostly to Gaza, the West Bank, and Lebanon; the UN Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA) was created in December 1949. In Iran, Prime Minister Mohammad Sa'ed's government was contending with parliamentary opposition led by Mohammad Mossadegh over the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. Syria had endured three military coups in 1949 alone.

Africa in 1949 was moving unevenly towards independence. In the Gold Coast, Kwame Nkrumah broke with the moderate United Gold Coast Convention on 12 June to found the Convention People's Party demanding 'self-government now'; strikes and protests would lead to his brief imprisonment in 1950. In South Africa the National Party government that had taken power in 1948 was passing the founding apartheid laws (Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act, Population Registration Act, Group Areas Act) between 1949 and 1950. In North Africa the Wafd was returning to power in Egypt on a platform of ending British occupation of the Canal Zone.

Australia in October 1949 was in the middle of a general election campaign that would bring Robert Menzies's Liberal Party to power on 10 December, ending eight years of Labor rule and beginning what became sixteen consecutive years of Liberal-Country Party government. The Colombo Plan for economic development in South and Southeast Asia was in early planning. In New Zealand, the first Labour government's welfare state remained intact under Peter Fraser but would soon give way to a National government under Sidney Holland.

figures.name: Mao Zedong
figures.role: Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party, proclaimed the founding of the People's Republic and served as its head of state until 1959 and chairman of the party until his death in 1976
figures.credit: Chen Zhengqing, 1950 official portrait, Public Domain (US)

figures.name: Zhou Enlai
figures.role: First premier and foreign minister of the People's Republic of China

figures.name: Zhu De
figures.role: Commander-in-chief of the People's Liberation Army, veteran of the Red Army since the 1920s

figures.name: Liu Shaoqi
figures.role: Senior CCP leader and vice-chairman of the Central People's Government Council; later head of state until purged in the Cultural Revolution

figures.name: Chiang Kai-shek
figures.role: Leader of the defeated Nationalist government of the Republic of China, evacuated to Taiwan in December 1949
figures.credit: Official portrait 1955, Office of the President of the ROC

figures.name: Song Qingling
figures.role: Widow of Sun Yat-sen, vice-chairwoman of the new Central People's Government Council

images.caption: Mao Zedong proclaims the founding of the People's Republic of China from the Gate of Heavenly Peace on 1 October 1949.
images.credit: Hou Bo, official Xinhua photographer, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The five-star flag of the People's Republic of China, adopted at the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference in September 1949.
images.credit: Government of the People's Republic of China, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen) in Beijing, from which the People's Republic was proclaimed on 1 October 1949 and above which Mao's portrait still hangs.
